metadata.visit_title: GI clinic — pre-procedure consult for EUS of incidental 14 mm pancreatic cyst

planted_discrepancies.chart_state: Condition 'Malignant tumor of pancreas' carried from referral text 'rule out malignancy' (verificationStatus=unconfirmed)
planted_discrepancies.spoken_truth: asymptomatic incidental 14 mm cyst found on trauma CT; malignancy is the QUESTION the EUS is meant to answer, not a diagnosis the patient carries
planted_discrepancies.note: Do NOT document pancreatic malignancy as an active/confirmed problem. 'Rule out malignancy' is the reason for the study; the working problem is a pancreatic cystic lesion of undetermined significance.

planted_discrepancies.chart_state: Medication list = None (no MedicationRequest on file)
planted_discrepancies.spoken_truth: patient takes OTC fish oil daily and asks if it is a 'blood thinner'; not a prescription anticoagulant
planted_discrepancies.note: Fish oil is not a therapeutic anticoagulant and does not require periprocedural hold, but should be captured before EUS with possible FNA and clarified for the patient. Do not miscode as an anticoagulant.

planted_discrepancies.spoken_truth: father had pancreatic cancer (diagnosed in his 70s)
planted_discrepancies.note: Family history — belongs to the father, NOT a diagnosis for this patient. Relevant risk factor that raises pretest concern for the cyst but must not be attributed to the patient.

planted_discrepancies.chart_state: Incidental 14 mm pancreatic cyst on trauma CT
planted_discrepancies.spoken_truth: patient confirms the cyst was an incidental finding after a bicycle accident and he has no abdominal symptoms
patient confirms he takes no prescription medications
patient confirms no chronic medical problems and no prior abdominal surgery

prior_notes.text: 48-year-old man presenting after a low-speed bicycle fall onto the left flank. Hemodynamically stable throughout. CT abdomen/pelvis with contrast obtained for trauma showed no solid-organ injury, no free fluid, and no fracture. Incidental note of a 14 mm cystic lesion in the body of the pancreas, not further characterized on this study. No abdominal tenderness, normal lipase. Discharged home with soft-tissue contusion. Advised outpatient GI referral for characterization of the incidental pancreatic cyst; MRCP or EUS recommended per radiology. No symptoms attributable to the pancreas.

Patient seen to review ED findings after bicycle accident. Feeling well, flank soreness resolving. Reviewed incidental 14 mm pancreatic cyst noted on trauma CT. Patient is asymptomatic — no abdominal pain, weight loss, jaundice, steatorrhea, or new-onset diabetes. Takes no prescription medications; reports occasional over-the-counter fish oil. Notes family history of pancreatic cancer in his father. Referring to Gastroenterology for EUS to characterize the cyst and rule out malignancy. Basic labs including CA 19-9, lipase, and bilirubin ordered.

code.text: Incidental 14 mm cystic lesion, body of pancreas (undetermined significance)
Pancreatic malignancy — TO BE RULED OUT (referral question, not an established diagnosis)

Condition._discrepancy: D1: this is the diagnostic QUESTION carried from referral text 'rule out malignancy' — verificationStatus is unconfirmed and must not be treated as a confirmed diagnosis

Blood pressure panel with all children optional

code.text: Endoscopic ultrasound of pancreas, cyst protocol (planned; FNA with fluid analysis if indicated)

coding.display: CT Abdomen and Pelvis W contrast

DiagnosticReport.conclusion: No traumatic solid-organ injury, no free fluid, no fracture. Incidental 14 mm cystic lesion in the body of the pancreas without associated mass, ductal dilation, or lymphadenopathy. Recommend dedicated characterization with MRCP or EUS. No CT features diagnostic of malignancy.

transcript:
DR: Good afternoon, Mr. Villanueva — thanks for coming in. I've read your file. This all started with the bicycle accident last month?
MA: Before the doctor gets started, let me grab your vitals — you're 5 foot 10 and 181 pounds today. Blood pressure 122 over 78, heart rate 72, temp 36.8, breathing 14, and your oxygen is 98 percent on room air.
PT: Yes. I went over the handlebars, landed on my side. They did a CT scan in the ER to make sure nothing was torn. Everything was fine — but they found this little spot on my pancreas by accident.
DR: That's right. A 14 millimeter cyst in the body of the pancreas, picked up incidentally. Just so I understand — have you had any belly pain, weight loss, yellowing of the eyes, changes in your stools, or new diabetes?
PT: No, nothing. Honestly I feel completely normal. If I hadn't crashed my bike I'd never have known it was there.
DR: That's very common — these are often found by chance. The referral says 'rule out malignancy,' and I want to be clear about what that means: it's the question we're trying to answer, not something you've been diagnosed with. Nothing on the CT says cancer. The scan is to characterize the cyst and make sure it's a benign type.
PT: Okay, that's a relief. My father had pancreatic cancer, so when I saw that word I got scared.
DR: I understand. That family history is worth noting — it's about your father, not a diagnosis for you, but it does make me want a careful look. When was he diagnosed?
PT: In his seventies, I think.
DR: Good to know. Now, any medications?
PT: No prescriptions at all. I do take fish oil every morning — is that a blood thinner? Should I stop it?
DR: Fish oil isn't a true blood thinner and won't stop the procedure. I'll note it, but you don't need to hold it. Any other medical problems or past surgeries?
PT: None. Never been in the hospital before this.
DR: Perfect. The plan is an endoscopic ultrasound — a scope that looks at the pancreas from the inside. If the cyst looks like it needs it, we sample the fluid with a fine needle during the same procedure. My team will call with prep and timing.
PT: Sounds good. Thank you for explaining it.
note:
**Subjective:** Mr. Óscar Villanueva is a 48-year-old man referred for pre-procedure evaluation of an incidental 14 mm pancreatic cystic lesion discovered on a trauma CT (2026-06-21) after a low-speed bicycle fall. He is asymptomatic: denies abdominal pain, weight loss, jaundice, steatorrhea, or new-onset diabetes. He takes no prescription medications and reports daily over-the-counter fish oil, which he asked about as a possible blood thinner. He has no chronic medical problems and no prior abdominal surgery. He volunteers a family history of pancreatic cancer in his father (diagnosed in his 70s).

**Objective:** Vitals (today): T 36.8 C, HR 72, RR 14, BP 122/78, SpO2 98% on RA; Ht 178 cm (5'10"), Wt 82 kg (181 lb), BMI 25.9. Labs (2026-07-10): CA 19-9 17 U/mL (normal), lipase 38 U/L (normal), total bilirubin 0.7 mg/dL, hemoglobin 15.1 g/dL. Trauma CT (2026-06-21): 14 mm cystic lesion in the pancreatic body without mass, ductal dilation, or lymphadenopathy; no CT features diagnostic of malignancy.

**Assessment & Plan:**
- Incidental 14 mm pancreatic cystic lesion of undetermined significance — asymptomatic. Malignancy is NOT established; 'rule out malignancy' is the referral question. Plan cyst-protocol EUS to characterize, with FNA and cyst-fluid analysis (CEA, cytology, and molecular testing) if the lesion meets criteria.
- Family history of pancreatic cancer (father) — noted as a risk factor; not a diagnosis for the patient.
- Medications: no prescription anticoagulants. OTC fish oil documented; not a therapeutic anticoagulant — no periprocedural hold required. Patient reassured.
- No significant PMH; no prior abdominal surgery.
- EUS scheduling, bowel-prep-free procedure instructions, and NPO/anesthesia guidance to follow. Return precautions for new abdominal pain, jaundice, or fever.
after_visit_summary:
Visit summary

What we discussed
• A small cyst on your pancreas that was found by chance on your accident CT scan
• You have no symptoms from it
• The plan for an endoscopic ultrasound (EUS) to take a closer look
• Your family history and your questions about fish oil

What this means
• The word 'malignancy' on your referral is the question we are checking — not a diagnosis. Nothing on your scan points to cancer.

Next steps
• We will schedule an endoscopic ultrasound to look closely at the cyst. If needed, we can sample the cyst fluid with a tiny needle during the same procedure.
• You do NOT need to stop your fish oil — it is not a blood thinner.
• Continue taking no prescription medications, as before.
• The team will call with your procedure date, timing, and instructions about not eating beforehand.
• Call us for new belly pain, yellowing of the skin or eyes, or fever.
